title: Herb Scissors for Outdoor BBQs: Fast Chili and Taco Toppings
meta_description: Stop chopping on the patio! Learn how to use herb scissors for outdoor BBQ toppings to get fresh cilantro and basil on your tacos in seconds.
html:
There is nothing worse than having a perfectly charred flank steak or a tray of street tacos ready to go, only to realize you still have to go back inside to mince a bunch of cilantro. When you're hosting outside, every trip back into the kitchen is a missed moment with your guests. This is where using herb scissors for outdoor BBQ toppings completely changes the game.

Most of us are used to the classic knife-and-board setup. While that works for a formal dinner, it's clunky for a BBQ. Carrying a cutting board, a chef's knife, and three different bunches of herbs across a deck is a recipe for a spill. A pair of multi-blade shears allows you to prep right at the serving station, keeping the herbs fresh and the workflow fast.

Why Multi-Blade Shears Beat the Cutting Board

The primary struggle with herbs like cilantro, parsley, and chives is that they are lightweight and slippery. When you use a standard knife, you spend half your time fighting the herbs to keep them in a pile. With the Herb Scissors, you have five parallel blades working simultaneously.

Instead of twenty individual chops with a knife, you get a finely minced result in a single snip. The five-blade design is specifically engineered for soft-stemmed herbs, meaning you can go from a whole stem to a sprinkle of garnish in about four seconds. This is a massive time-saver when you're juggling a grill and a crowd.

Perfecting Your Taco and Chili Toppings

Tacos and chili are all about the contrast between heavy, savory proteins and bright, acidic toppings. The key is the texture of the herbs; if they are hacked into uneven chunks, they don't distribute evenly across the dish.

For street tacos, use your Herb Scissors to quickly mince cilantro and white onion. Because the blades cut cleanly, you avoid bruising the leaves, which keeps that vibrant green color and sharp flavor intact. For a pot of outdoor chili, a quick snip of chives or scallions adds a professional finish without the need for a prep station.

Here are the best herbs to prep with these shears for your next BBQ:

  • Cilantro: Perfect for tacos, corn elotes, and lime rice.
  • Chives: Ideal for loaded baked potatoes or topping a grilled steak.
  • Basil: Great for bruschetta or topping a grilled mozzarella and tomato platter.
  • Parsley: The universal garnish for grilled chicken and seafood.

Using Herb Scissors for Outdoor BBQ Toppings Efficiency

To make the most of your outdoor setup, I recommend a "topping station." Place your whole herb bunches in small glasses of water to keep them crisp. Keep your Herb Scissors right next to them.

Instead of prepping everything in advance—which often leads to the herbs wilting or turning black—you can snip them directly onto the plates. If you're serving a large group, you can snip a mountain of fresh basil directly into a bowl of cherry tomatoes and olive oil in seconds, ensuring the flavors are as potent as possible.

Cleaning and Maintenance on the Fly

One of the biggest complaints about multi-blade shears is that herbs tend to get stuck between the blades, especially with "sticky" herbs like basil. This is why the cleaning comb included with the Herb Scissors is essential.

You don't need to run back to the kitchen sink every time you switch from cilantro to chives. A quick swipe with the cleaning comb clears out the debris, allowing you to switch toppings instantly. When the party is over, these are generally dishwasher safe, but a quick rinse and a dry with a towel will keep the blades from rusting in humid outdoor environments.

Tips for the Best Cut

To get the most out of your shears, make sure your herbs are completely dry before you start snipping. If the leaves are wet, they tend to clump together or stick to the blades, which reduces the efficiency of the five-blade system. Pat them down with a paper towel first.

Also, avoid using these on woody stems like rosemary or thick cinnamon sticks. These are precision tools designed for soft herbs. Stick to the leafy greens and chives, and you'll find that your prep time is cut by more than half.

FAQ

Can I use these scissors for thicker vegetable stems?

It's best to avoid thick, woody stems. These shears are optimized for soft herbs like cilantro and basil. Using them on thick stalks can dull the blades or cause them to misalign.

How do I stop the herbs from sticking to the blades?

Use the included cleaning comb frequently between cuts. Additionally, ensuring your herbs are dry before cutting prevents them from clumping and sticking to the metal.

Are multi-blade scissors better than a food processor?

For small quantities and garnishes, yes. A food processor often over-processes herbs into a paste or requires too much cleanup for just a handful of cilantro. Scissors provide a clean cut and zero cleanup.
